content:
# Ephesians Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.1 — "the prisoner in the Lord" vs. "a prisoner for the Lord" vs. "a prisoner for serving the Lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I the prisoner in [the] Lord" | ho desmios en tō kyriō — "the prisoner in the Lord" |
| LOGOS | "a prisoner for the Lord" | "For" — purpose/cause |
| PNEUMA | "a prisoner for serving the Lord" | Adds "serving" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. en kyriō could be locative ("in the Lord") or causal ("for the Lord's sake"). LOGOS's "for the Lord" captures the sense. PNEUMA adds "serving" — not in Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "serving."

---

### v.4 — "one hope of your calling" vs. "one glorious hope for the future"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in one hope of your calling" | en mia elpidi tēs klēseōs hymōn |
| LOGOS | "to the one hope that belongs to your call" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "one glorious hope for the future" | Adds "glorious" and "for the future" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says "the hope of your calling" — the hope that belongs to the calling. PNEUMA adds "glorious" (not in Greek) and replaces "of your calling" with "for the future."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds and replaces.

---

### v.8 — Psalm 68:18 quotation: "captivity captive" vs. "a host of captives" vs. "a crowd of captives"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he led captivity captive and gave gifts to men" | ēchmalōteusen aichmalōsian — cognate accusative |
| LOGOS | "he led a host of captives, and he gave gifts to men" | "Host of captives" — interprets abstract as concrete |
| PNEUMA | "he led a crowd of captives and gave gifts to his people" | "Crowd"; "his people" for anthrōpois |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The cognate "captivity captive" is awkward in English; LOGOS renders concretely. PNEUMA changes "men" (anthrōpois) to "his people" — adding a possessive not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds possessive.

---

### v.9 — "the lower parts of the earth" vs. "the lower regions, the earth" vs. "our lowly world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he also descended into the lower parts of the earth" | ta katōtera merē tēs gēs — partitive genitive |
| LOGOS | "he had also descended into the lower regions, the earth" | Epexegetical — "the earth" explains "lower regions" |
| PNEUMA | "Christ also descended to our lowly world" | "Our lowly world" — paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The phrase is genuinely ambiguous — "the lower parts of the earth" (Hades?) or "the lower regions, namely the earth" (incarnation?). LOGOS's epexegetical rendering favors the incarnation reading while preserving the phrase. PNEUMA's "our lowly world" resolves the ambiguity and adds "our."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Christ's "descent" — to Hades (descensus ad inferos) or to earth (incarnation)? This affects christological doctrine and creedal interpretation. The consensus follows the incarnation reading.

---

### v.11 — "shepherds and teachers" vs. "pastors and teachers"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "some shepherds and teachers" | tous de poimenas kai didaskalous |
| LOGOS | "the shepherds and teachers" | Same — one article governing both |
| PNEUMA | "the pastors and teachers" | "Pastors" for poimenas |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. poimēn means "shepherd" — "pastor" is its Latin equivalent (pastor = shepherd). Both are valid. LOGOS preserves the pastoral metaphor; PNEUMA uses the ecclesiastical term. The single article (tous de) before both nouns suggests they may describe one office.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — "shepherds" vs. "pastors."

---

### v.13 — "unto a mature man" vs. "to mature manhood" vs. "mature in the Lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unto a mature man, unto [the] measure of [the] stature of the fullness of the Christos" | eis andra teleion |
| LOGOS | "to mature manhood, to the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ" | "Mature manhood" — abstract |
| PNEUMA | "mature in the Lord, measuring up to the full and complete standard of Christ" | "Mature in the Lord"; "standard" for stature |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. eis andra teleion is "unto a complete/mature man" — the corporate body reaching full human maturity in Christ. PNEUMA changes "the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ" into "the full and complete standard of Christ" — "standard" replaces the physical metaphor of stature.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses stature metaphor.

---

### v.17 — "in the futility of their minds" vs. "hopelessly confused"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in [the] futility of their mind" | en mataiotēti tou noos autōn |
| LOGOS | "in the futility of their minds" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "for they are hopelessly confused" | "Hopelessly confused" — paraphrase; adds "hopelessly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. mataiotēs means "futility/emptiness/vanity" — a LXX echo (cf. Ecclesiastes). PNEUMA changes to "hopelessly confused" — losing the futility/vanity concept and adding "hopelessly."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses mataiotēs concept.

---

### v.22-24 — "old man/new man" vs. "old self/new self" vs. "old sinful nature/new nature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to put off...the old man...to put on the new man" | ton palaion anthrōpon...ton kainon anthrōpon |
| LOGOS | "to put off your old self...to put on the new self" | "Self" — avoids gendered "man" |
| PNEUMA | "throw off your old sinful nature...put on your new nature" | "Sinful nature" / "new nature" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA again renders anthrōpos as "nature" (cf. Galatians 5, sarx as "sinful nature") — importing the idea that what is put off is an internal nature rather than an entire mode of existence. Paul's language is of clothing — putting off and putting on a whole person, not an inner component.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "Old self/new self" (whole person) vs. "old sinful nature/new nature" (inner component). Same anthropological issue as Galatians 5 — Paul envisions a change of entire existence, not a swap of inner natures.

---

### v.26 — "Be angry and do not sin" vs. "don't sin by letting anger control you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Be angry and do not sin" | orgizesthe kai mē hamartanete — two imperatives |
| LOGOS | "Be angry and do not sin" | Same — preserves both imperatives |
| PNEUMA | "don't sin by letting anger control you" | Subordinates anger to sin; adds "control you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek has two coordinate imperatives: "be angry" AND "do not sin." PNEUMA subordinates the first to the second, making anger the condition of sin rather than a permitted emotion. Paul (quoting Psalm 4:4) permits anger while prohibiting sinful expression of it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes the relationship between anger and sin.

---

### v.29 — "corrupt word" vs. "corrupting talk" vs. "foul or abusive language"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Let no corrupt word go out from your mouth" | logos sapros — "rotten word" |
| LOGOS | "Let no corrupting talk come out of your mouths" | "Corrupting" — active sense |
| PNEUMA | "Don't use foul or abusive language" | "Foul or abusive" — doubles the adjective |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. sapros means "rotten/putrid" — originally used for rotten fruit or fish. "Corrupting" captures the active sense. PNEUMA doubles to "foul or abusive" — two adjectives where Greek has one.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — PNEUMA doubles.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 4 | "Serving," "glorious," "hopelessly," "his people" |
| PNEUMA nature/self language | 1 | "Sinful nature/new nature" for old/new anthrōpos (vv.22-24) |
| Christ's descent ambiguity | 1 | Hades vs. incarnation (v.9) |
| Anger imperative restructuring | 1 | Coordinate imperatives vs. subordination (v.26) |
| PNEUMA paraphrase | 2 | "Lowly world" (v.9), "hopelessly confused" (v.17) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 4's most significant disagreements are the descent/ascent christology (v.9 — Hades or incarnation?) and PNEUMA's continued rendering of anthrōpos as "nature" rather than "self/person" (vv.22-24). The "nature" rendering imports a component anthropology that differs from Paul's clothing/identity metaphor. The anger imperative (v.26) is also notable — Paul permits anger; PNEUMA subordinates it.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
